title: Arturo Alfonso Schomburg Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Arturo Alfonso Schomburg Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a special gold medal to honor Arturo Alfonso Schomburg, a man from Puerto Rico who spent his life collecting and saving important items about African and African American history and culture. The medal will be put on display at the National Museum of African American History and Culture so people can see it. The government can also make bronze copies of the medal to sell to the public. This medal is a way to recognize and remember Schomburg's important work in preserving Black history.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on House Administration, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Arturo Alfonso Schomburg Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts about Arturo Alfonso Schomburg. He was born on January twenty fourth eighteen seventy four in Santurce, Puerto Rico. His father was Puerto Rican with German roots and his mother was Afro-Caribbean from St. Croix. When Schomburg was young, a teacher told him that people of African background had no history, heroes, or achievements. This statement inspired him to spend his life proving this wrong by collecting proof of what Africans and their children accomplished. When he was seventeen years old, Schomburg moved to New York City. There he became an active member of the smart community during the Harlem Renaissance, which was a time when African and African American culture and history grew popular. Schomburg gathered a personal collection of over ten thousand items related to Black history and people of African background around the world. He made this collection available for the public to see. His collection included original newspapers published by Frederick Douglass, poems by Phillis Wheatley, letters from Toussaint Louverture, books and journals by Paul Cuffe, and music written by Chevalier de Saint-Georges. During the nineteen twenties, Schomburg was an active member of the Negro Society for Historical Research and the American Negro Academy. He wrote about the history of African people around the world and Cuban poets of African background. In nineteen twenty six, the New York Public Library bought Schomburg's collection. This collection became the foundation for the Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture in Harlem. This center is now a world-famous place for studying the global Black experience. Schomburg's collection has grown to more than ten million items. Schomburg's work has inspired many generations of scholars, writers, and artists to explore and celebrate the rich history and culture of African people around the world. The Congressional Gold Medal would be a good way to honor Schomburg's legacy and his role in building the foundation for future learning and worldwide recognition of Black contributions to society.

sections.title: Section 3  Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the person who leads the Senate when the Vice President is not there) must make proper arrangements to give a gold medal after death to Arturo Alfonso Schomburg on behalf of Congress. This medal recognizes his groundbreaking work in collecting and preserving the history and culture of people of African background around the world. The Secretary of the Treasury (the person who runs the Treasury Department) must create a gold medal with suitable symbols, designs, and words that the Secretary decides on. After the gold medal is awarded, it must be given to the National Museum of African American History and Culture of the Smithsonian Institution. The museum will display it properly and make it available for research. Congress believes that the Smithsonian Institution should make the gold medal available for display in other places too, especially at locations and events connected to Arturo Alfonso Schomburg.

sections.title: Section 4  Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may make and sell copies in bronze of the gold medal described in section three. The price must be enough to cover the cost of making them, including worker pay, materials, tools for making coins, use of machines, and other business expenses.

sections.title: Section 5  Status of Medals
sections.text: The medals made under this law are national medals for the purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one, United States Code (a part of federal law about money and medals). For the purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one, United States Code, all medals made under this law shall be considered collectible coin items.

sections.title: Section 6  Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is allowed to charge the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund (a special government account for coin-making costs) for whatever amounts are needed to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from selling the duplicate bronze medals described in section four must be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
